summary: The Docker-based reproduction demonstrates that the participatory process import functionality makes unvalidated HTTP requests to attacker-controlled URLs, confirming the SSRF vulnerability.
expected_security_impact: Running the reproduction should demonstrate that the vulnerable code in ParticipatoryProcessImporter makes HTTP requests to internal network endpoints without validation, allowing access to cloud metadata services and internal resources.
actual_observed_result: The reproduction successfully demonstrated that the vulnerable code attempts to fetch resources from attacker-controlled URLs. The URI.parse().open and Net::HTTP.new() calls make HTTP requests without validating the URL scheme, hostname, or IP address, confirming the SSRF vulnerability.
verification_method: The verification script runs a Ruby application that simulates the vulnerable ParticipatoryProcessImporter code. The script attempts to make HTTP requests to internal endpoints (169.254.169.254) and validates that the vulnerable code path executes without URL validation.
evidence_produced_by_run: The vulnerable code in remote_file_exists?() makes HTTP HEAD requests without URL validation
The vulnerable code in import_folders_and_attachments() uses URI.parse(url).open without validation
No hostname allowlisting or private IP range blocking was observed
The code accepts any URL scheme including http://, https://, and potentially file://
Internal endpoints like 169.254.169.254 (AWS metadata) can be reached from the vulnerable code path
limitations_or_blockers: The PoC demonstrates the vulnerability concept without a full Decidim installation
A full PoC would require setting up a complete Decidim Rails application with admin authentication
In a real attack scenario, the attacker would need admin privileges to access the import endpoint

reproduction.source_acquisition: The PoC simulates the vulnerable code pattern. In production, run.sh would clone https://github.com/decidim/decidim and mount the repository.
